c741bfae547e.statement: For each $0 < \delta, \mu < \infty$, there exists $\underline{c}_{26}(n,k,\mu,\delta) < \delta$ such that if $V, W$ are stationary with $0 \leq V - W$ and $V$ has nearly-$d$-disk mass, then $W$ inherits the mass estimate: $(1-\delta)\alpha(k)(\underline{c}_{15}R)^k \leq \|W\|\underline{B}(a,\underline{c}_{15}R)$ and $\|W\|\underline{U}(a,R) \leq (1+\delta)\alpha(k)R^k$.
c741bfae547e.proof: By contradiction with compactness: sequences $V_i$, $W_i$ with $0 \leq V_i - W_i$ and mass bounds converging to equality yield limits with density and monotonicity properties forcing the mass bounds, contradiction.

646066fd856f.name: Regularity of Area Minimizing Hypersurfaces
646066fd856f.statement:
If $1 \leq k \leq 6$, $
u \geq 4$, $M$ is a properly imbedded $(k+1)$-dimensional submanifold of class $
u+1$ of $\mathbb{R}^n$, and $T \in \mathcal{R}_k(M)$ is homologically area minimizing with respect to $(M,\phi)$, then $\operatorname{spt} T \sim \operatorname{spt} \partial T$ is a minimal $k$-dimensional submanifold of class $
u$ of $M$.
646066fd856f.proof: In case $M = \mathbb{R}^{k+1}$, this strong regularity theorem for minimizing currents is just <objref id="af3f51d44df5">[FH1, 5.4.15]</objref>. The missing ingredient necessary to extend to manifolds is an appropriate maximum principle, a stronger version of <objref id="af3f51d44df5">[FH1, 5.4.4]</objref>. There is a suitable extension due to L. Simon and R. Schoen <objref id="65cf80198b75">[SSA]</objref> which is described below.

8a2d897501e4.name: Maximum Principle for Elliptic Integrands
8a2d897501e4.statement:
We adopt here the notation of <objref id="af3f51d44df5">[FH1, chapter 5]</objref>. Suppose $U$ is a connected, open subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$, $\phi$ is a positive, elliptic integrand of degree $n$ and class 3 on $U \times \mathbb{R}$,

$G = \phi^{\delta} : U \times \mathbb{R} \times \operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb{R}^n, \mathbb{R}) \to \mathbb{R}$

is the nonparametric integrand associated with $\phi$ (<objref id="af3f51d44df5">[FH1, 5.1.1, 5.1.9]</objref>), $f_i: U \to \mathbb{R}$ is of class 1 for $i = 1,2$,

$C = U \cap \{x : f_1(x) = f_2(x)\},$

and

$0 = \int_U \langle [0, \theta(x)], D\theta(x) \rangle \, DG[x, f_i(x), Df_i(x)] \, d\mathcal{L}^n x$

for all $\theta \in \delta(U)$ and $i = 1,2$.

(1) If $Df_1(x) = Df_2(x)$ for all $x \in C$, then either $C = U$ or $C = \emptyset$.

(2) If $f_1 \leq f_2$ on $U$, then either $C = U$ or $C = \emptyset$.
8a2d897501e4.proof: The functions $f_1$ and $f_2$ are of class 2 by 2.4(11). (1) follows from <objref id="65cf80198b75">[SSA, lemma 2.4]</objref>, and (2) follows from (1). Recalling the last paragraph of <objref id="af3f51d44df5">[FH1, 5.4.4]</objref>, we see that <objref id="af3f51d44df5">[FH1, 5.4.15]</objref> may be extended naturally to smooth, properly imbedded submanifolds of $\mathbb{R}^n$. In particular, we obtain 7.2.
8a2d897501e4.notes: Pitts 7.3. Due to L. Simon and R. Schoen [SSA].

d0cc16f31647.name: Regularity of Limits of Stable Manifolds
d0cc16f31647.statement:
Suppose $2 \leq k \leq 5$, $
u \geq 4$, $M$ is a properly imbedded $(k+1)$-dimensional submanifold of class $
u+1$ of $\mathbb{R}^n$, and $U$ is a bounded, relatively open subset of $M$. If $V, V_1, V_2, \ldots$ are varifolds in $\mathcal{J}
u_k(M,U)$, $\lim_{i 	o \infty} V_i = V$, $V_i$ is stable in $U$ for each $i = 1,2,\ldots$, and $U \cap \operatorname{spt} \|V_i\|$ is a $k$-dimensional submanifold of class 1 of $U$ for each $i = 1,2,\ldots$, then $U \cap \operatorname{spt} \|V\|$ is a $k$-dimensional submanifold of class $
u$ of $U$ which is stable in $U$.
d0cc16f31647.proof:
We take $M = \mathbb{R}^{k+1} = \mathbb{R}^n$ (ref. 7.1), and we let $a \in U \cap \operatorname{spt} \|V\|$ be arbitrary. Letting

$\mu = d = \Theta^k(\|V\|, a)$, $p = k+1$, and $4^{-1} > \varepsilon = \underline{c}_1(n,k,4^{-1},p) > 0$

($\underline{c}_1$ is the constant of the Allard regularity theorem), we choose $\underline{c}_{17}(n,k,\mu)$ and $\underline{c}_{18}(n,k,\mu,\varepsilon,p)$ as in the decomposition theorem (6.3). By hypothesis and monotonicity (2.4(3)), there exist a positive integer $J$ and a finite positive number $R$ sufficiently small that $\underline{B}(a,R) \subset U$ such that for all integers $i \geq J$,

$(1 - \underline{c}_{18}) \, d\alpha(k) \, (\underline{c}_{15}(n,k) \, \underline{c}_{17} \, R)^k \leq \|V_i\| \, \underline{B}(a, \underline{c}_{15}(n,k) \, \underline{c}_{17} \, R)$

and

$\|V_i\| \, \underline{U}(a,R) \leq (1 + \underline{c}_{18}) \, d\alpha(k) \, R^k.$

By 6.3(1), 6.3(2), and 6.3(4), there exist a positive integer $m$ and, for each $i \geq J$, a decomposition

$\mathcal{D}_i = \{D_i^1, \ldots, D_i^m\}$

of class $\nu$ of $V_i$ in $\underline{U}(a, \underline{c}_{17}R)$ such that

$\|D_i^j\| \, \underline{U}(a, \underline{c}_{17}R) \leq \alpha(k)(1 + \underline{c}_1)(\underline{c}_{17}R)^k$

for each $j = 1,2,\ldots,m$. Applying the compactness theorem (2.4(4)), we find a positive integer $q \leq m$, varifolds $D^1, \ldots, D^m$ in $\mathcal{J}\nu_k(\mathbb{R}^{k+1}, \underline{U}(a, \underline{c}_{17}R))$, and subsequences such that for each $j = 1,2,\ldots,m$,

$D^j$ is stationary in $\underline{U}(a, \underline{c}_{17}R)$,

$\|D_i^j\| \, \underline{U}(a, \underline{c}_{17}R) \leq \alpha(k)(1+\underline{c}_1)(\underline{c}_{17}R)^k$,

$\lim_{i \to \infty} D_i^j \restriction \mathbf{G}_k(\underline{U}(a, \underline{c}_{17}R)) = D^j \restriction \mathbf{G}_k(\underline{U}(a, \underline{c}_{17}R)),$

and

$\sum_{j=1}^{m} D^j \restriction \mathbf{G}_k(\underline{U}(a, \underline{c}_{17}R)) = V \restriction \mathbf{G}_k(\underline{U}(a, \underline{c}_{17}R)),$

$0 \in \operatorname{spt}\|D^j\|$ if and only if $1 \leq j \leq q$.

By the regularity theorem (2.4(8)), there exists a positive number $r$, $0 < r \leq \underline{c}_{17}R$, such that $\underline{U}(a,r) \cap \operatorname{spt}\|D^j\|$ is a minimal submanifold of class $\nu$ of $\mathbb{R}^{k+1}$ for each $j = 1,2,\ldots,q$; and, furthermore,

$\underline{B}(a,r) \cap \bigcup_{j=q+1}^{m} \operatorname{spt}\|D^j\| = \emptyset.$

It follows that

$\operatorname{VarTan}(V,a) = \left|\sum_{j=1}^{q} |\operatorname{Tan}(\operatorname{spt}\|D^j\|,a)|\right|,$

hence that

$\operatorname{Tan}(\operatorname{spt}\|D^i\|, a) = \operatorname{Tan}(\operatorname{spt}\|D^j\|, a)$

for all $\{i,j\} \subset \{1,2,\ldots,q\}$, since (by 6.6) $C = q|S|$ for some $S \in \mathbf{G}(n,k)$, whenever $C \in \operatorname{VarTan}(V,a)$. The point $a$ was arbitrary, so our conclusion obtains from 7.3(1).
d0cc16f31647.notes: Pitts 7.4. Uses decomposition theorem (6.3) in an essential way.

2bcee9d2839b.name: Disk Theorem for Stable Cones (Strengthened)
2bcee9d2839b.statement:
Suppose $2 \leq k \leq 5$, $0 < \mu < \infty$, $C \in \mathbf{IV}_k(\mathbb{R}^{k+1})$, $\underline{\mu}(r)_{\#}C = C$ for all finite positive numbers $r$, and $\|C\| \, \underline{B}(0,1) \leq \alpha(k)\mu$. We do not assume $\operatorname{spt}\|C\|$ is regular. If $V_1, V_2, \ldots$ is a sequence of varifolds in $\mathbf{IV}_k(\mathbb{R}^{k+1})$ which are stable in $\underline{A}_0(0, \underline{c}_{28}(k,\mu), 1)$,

$\lim_{j \to \infty} V_j \restriction \mathbf{G}_k(\underline{A}_0(0, \underline{c}_{28}, 1)) = C \restriction \mathbf{G}_k(\underline{A}_0(0, \underline{c}_{28}, 1)),$

and $\underline{A}_0(0, \underline{c}_{28}, 1) \cap \operatorname{spt}\|V_j\|$ is a $k$-dimensional submanifold of class 1 of $\mathbb{R}^{k+1}$ for each $j = 1,2,\ldots$, then $C = m|S|$ for some positive integer $m$ and some plane $S \in \mathbf{G}(k+1,k)$. In fact $m = \Theta^k(\|C\|, 0)$.
2bcee9d2839b.proof: According to <objref id="4585a5d406ad"></objref> and <objref id="d0cc16f31647"></objref>, $\underline{A}_0(0, \underline{c}_{28}, 1) \cap \operatorname{spt}\|C\|$ is a real analytic submanifold of $\mathbb{R}^{k+1}$; so the same is true of $\operatorname{spt}\|C\| \sim \{0\}$ since $C$ is homothetically invariant. Furthermore, $C$ is stable in $\underline{A}_0(0, \underline{c}_{28}, 1)$. This contradicts the theorem above.

0af8785a27d6.name: Regularity of Comparison Surfaces
0af8785a27d6.statement: Let $k, M, U$, and $\nu$ be as in 7.4. If $K$ is a compact subset of $U$, $V$ is almost minimizing in $U$, and $V^* \in \mathfrak{B}(V; U, K)$, then $(\operatorname{Int} K) \cap \operatorname{spt}\|V^*\|$ is a $k$-dimensional submanifold of class $\nu$ of $M$ which is stable in $\operatorname{Int}(K)$.
0af8785a27d6.proof:
By <objref id="dfb777a7655b"></objref>(4), there exists a sequence $T_1, T_2, \ldots$ in $\tilde{\mathcal{Z}}_k(M, M \sim U)$ such that for each positive integer $j$ and each compact, Lipschitz neighborhood retract $Z \subset K$, $T_j$ is locally area minimizing with respect to $(Z, \phi)$, and such that

$\lim_{j \to \infty} |T_j| \restriction \mathbf{G}_k(U) = V^* \restriction \mathbf{G}_k(U).$

For each $j$, it follows that $|T_j|$ is stable in $\operatorname{Int}(K)$ and $(\operatorname{spt} T_j) \cap \operatorname{Int}(K)$ is a submanifold of class $\nu$. The conclusion follows from <objref id="d0cc16f31647"></objref>.
0af8785a27d6.notes: Pitts 7.7. First regularity result for almost minimizing varifolds, via comparison surfaces.

0c4f1fe7a3be.name: Tangent Cones of Almost Minimizing Varifolds are Planes
0c4f1fe7a3be.statement: If $2 \leq k \leq 5$, $V$ is almost minimizing in $\underline{A}_0(p,s,r) \cap M$ for all $0 < s < r$, and $C \in \operatorname{VarTan}(V,p)$, then $\Theta^k(\|V\|, p)$ is a positive integer and $C = \Theta^k(\|V\|, p) |S|$ for some plane $S \in \mathbf{G}(n,k) \cap \{P : P \subset \operatorname{Tan}(M,p)\}$.
0c4f1fe7a3be.proof: Take $p = 0$. Choose comparison surfaces $V_i \in \mathfrak{B}(V; M \cap \underline{A}_0(0, \frac{t}{2}r_i, 2r_i), M \cap \underline{A}(0, tr_i, r_i))$ with $t = \underline{c}_{28}(k, \Theta^k(\|V\|, 0))$. By 3.11, limits $C$ and $D$ are integral varifold cones with $C = D$. By 7.7, the rescaled comparison surfaces have regular support in annuli. By 7.4, the limit $D$ is regular in $\underline{A}_0(0, t, 1)$. Apply 7.6' to conclude $C = m|S|$.
0c4f1fe7a3be.notes: Pitts 7.8. Does not prove uniqueness of tangent cones; that is handled in 7.12.

5b01b8db766e.name: Stationary Cone Rigidity
5b01b8db766e.statement: If $C \in \mathbf{V}_k(\mathbb{R}^n)$ is stationary with $\Theta^k(\|C\|,x) \geq d$ a.e., $\|C\|\underline{U}(0,s) = r\alpha(k)s^k$ for all $s > 0$, $P \in \mathbf{G}(n,k)$, and $\Theta^k(\|C\|,x) = r$ for $\|C\|$-a.e. $x$ in a set $K \subset P$ with $\mathcal{H}^k(K) > 0$, then $C = r|P|$.
5b01b8db766e.proof: By monotonicity and the cone property, $\|C\|\underline{U}(x,s) \leq r\alpha(k)s^k$ for all $x$. Since $K \subset P$ is rectifiable with positive measure, choose $x \in K$ with $\operatorname{Tan}^k(\|C\|,x) = P$, giving $\operatorname{VarTan}(C,x) = \{r|P|\}$. By 2.4(6), $C$ is a cone with vertex at $x$; the conclusion follows.

6a8064c29f32.name: Comparison Surface Continuation Lemma
6a8064c29f32.statement: If $2 \leq k \leq 5$, $V$ is almost minimizing in $N \cap \underline{A}_0(p,t_3,t_1)$, and $\underline{A}_0(p,t_2,t_1) \cap \operatorname{spt}\|V\|$ is regular, then for a.e. $s \in (t_2,t_1)$: if $V^* \in \mathfrak{B}(V; N \cap \underline{A}_0(p,t_3,t_1), N \cap \underline{A}(p,t,s))$ for any $t_3 < t < t_2$, then $V^* = V$ in $\underline{A}_0(p,t_2,s)$.
6a8064c29f32.proof: The proof is in 10 parts. Near each boundary point $x_0 \in B_1 = M_0 \cap \partial\underline{B}(0,s)$, the decomposition theorem (6.3) and Allard's regularity give an $m$-sheeted decomposition of $V^*$. Each sheet has the same boundary $B_1$, so by the maximum principle (7.3(1)) all sheets coincide. Covering $B_1$ by small balls and using compactness gives $V^* = V$ in the annular region.
6a8064c29f32.notes: Pitts 7.10. Critical technical bridge between almost minimizing varifolds and their comparison surfaces.

7898ad0edfdd.name: Regularity of Almost Minimizing Varifolds in a Disk
7898ad0edfdd.statement:
If $2 \leq k \leq 5$, $
u \geq \max\{4,k\}$, $M$ is a properly imbedded $(k+1)$-dimensional submanifold of class $(
u+1)$, and $V$ is almost minimizing in the bounded relatively open set $U \subset M$, then $U \cap \operatorname{spt}\|V\|$ is a $k$-dimensional submanifold of class $
u$ of $M$.
7898ad0edfdd.proof: Fix $p \in U \cap \operatorname{spt}\|V\|$. Choose $r$ small, fix $t_1 > t_2 > 0$, and let $V^* \in \mathfrak{B}(V; U, M \cap \underline{A}(p,t_2,t_1))$. By 7.7, $V^*$ is regular in the annulus. Choose $s$ satisfying 7.10, then $W \in \mathfrak{B}(V^*; U, M \cap \underline{B}(p,s))$. By 7.7, $W$ is regular in $\underline{U}(p,s)$. By 7.10, $V^* = V$ in $\underline{A}_0(p,t_2,s)$, hence $\operatorname{spt}\|V\| \subset M_2 = \underline{U}(p,s) \cap \operatorname{spt}\|W\|$ near $p$.
7898ad0edfdd.notes: Pitts 7.11. Uses 7.7 and 7.10 in a continuation argument.

4ef2774c22b5.name: Regularity of Almost Minimizing Varifolds in Annuli
4ef2774c22b5.statement:
If $2 \leq k \leq 5$, $
u \geq \max\{4,k\}$, $N$ is a properly imbedded $(k+1)$-dimensional submanifold of class $(
u+1)$ of $\mathbb{R}^n$, $p \in N$, $0 < r < \infty$, $V \in 
u_k(N)$, $V$ is stationary in $N \cap \underline{U}(p,r)$, and $V$ is almost minimizing in $N \cap \underline{A}_0(p,s,r)$ for all numbers $s$, $0 < s < r$, then $\underline{U}(p,r) \cap \operatorname{spt}\|V\|$ is a $k$-dimensional minimal submanifold of class $
u$ of $N \cap \underline{U}(p,r)$.
4ef2774c22b5.proof:
**Part 1.** Assume $p \in \operatorname{spt}\|V\|$. By 7.11, it suffices to show that $p$ is a regular point of $\operatorname{spt}\|V\|$. The proof is local, and as noted in 7.1, there is no loss in assuming that $N = \mathbb{R}^{k+1} = \mathbb{R}^n$; and using $\underline{\mu}(r^{-1}) \cdot \underline{\tau}(p)$, we may take $r = 1$ and $p = 0$. Thus, $V$ is almost minimizing in $\underline{A}_0(0,s,1)$ for all $s$, $0 < s < 1$. By 7.10 and 3.3 we know that

$M = \underline{A}_0(0,0,1) \cap \operatorname{spt}\|V\|$

is a $k$-dimensional submanifold of class $\nu$ of $\mathbb{R}^{k+1}$, and $V$ is stable in $\underline{A}_0(0,0,1)$.

The main technical problem is that tangent cones of $V$ at $p$ are not known a priori to be unique. We do have 7.8, however, so there are a positive integer $m$ and a function

$P: \operatorname{VarTan}(V,0) \to \mathbf{G}(k+1,k)$

such that $P(C) = \operatorname{spt}\|C\|$ and $C = m|P(C)|$ for all $C \in \operatorname{VarTan}(V,0)$.

**Part 2.** Let us write $\underline{c}_1 = \underline{c}_1(k+1, k, 2^{-1}, k+1)$, which is the constant of the Allard regularity theorem with $\varepsilon = 2^{-1}$ and $p = k+1$. There is a number $z_1$, with $0 < z_1 < 1$, such that if $Z \neq \emptyset$ is a $k$-dimensional submanifold of class $\nu$ of $\mathbb{R}^{k+1}$, $0 < \delta < 1$, $S \in \mathbf{G}(k+1,k)$,

$(\operatorname{Clos}(Z) \sim Z) \cap \underline{A}_0(0, 1-\delta, 1+\delta) = \emptyset,$

$\|\operatorname{Tan}(Z,x) - S\| \leq z_1$ for all $x \in Z$, and

$Z \subset \{x : |S^{\perp}(x)| \leq z_1\}$,

then $B = Z \cap \partial\underline{B}(0,1)$ is a nonempty, compact, connected, $(k-1)$-dimensional submanifold of class 2 of $\mathbb{R}^{k+1}$, and

$|\mathcal{H}^{k-1}(B) - k\alpha(k)| \leq \underline{c}_1.$

**Part 3.** There are positive numbers $z_2$, $z_3$, with $0 < z_3 < 1$, such that if $S \in \mathbf{G}(k+1,k)$, $0 < r < \infty$, and

$\underline{F}_{\underline{U}(0,3)}(\underline{\mu}(r^{-1})_{\#}V, m|S|) \leq z_2,$

then

$\underline{A}_0(0, 1 - z_3, 1 + z_3) \cap \operatorname{spt}\|\underline{\mu}(r^{-1})_{\#}V\| \subset \{x : |S^{\perp}(x)| \leq z_1\},$

$\|\operatorname{Tan}(\operatorname{spt}\|\underline{\mu}(r^{-1})_{\#}V\|, x) - S\| \leq z_1,$

$\underline{\mu}(r^{-1})_{\#}V$ has an $m$-sheeted decomposition of class $\nu$ above $S$ in $\underline{A}_0(0, 1-z_3, 1+z_3)$ such that if two sheets intersect, then they coincide.

**Part 4.** $\operatorname{VarTan}(V,0)$ is compact in the weak topology. It follows that there is a number $z_5$, with $0 < z_5 < 1$, such that if $0 < r \leq z_5$, then

$\underline{F}_{\underline{U}(0,3)}(\underline{\mu}(r^{-1})_{\#}V, C) \leq z_2$

for some $C \in \operatorname{VarTan}(V,0)$.

**Part 5.** Combining parts 3 and 4, it follows that for each $r$, $0 < r \leq z_5$, there exist a cone $C \in \operatorname{VarTan}(V,0)$ and a plane $P(C) \in \mathbf{G}(k+1,k)$ such that $V$ has an $m$-sheeted decomposition of class $\nu$ in $\underline{A}_0(0, r(1-z_3), r(1+z_3))$ over $P(C)$. As a result, there are $k$-dimensional submanifolds $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_m$ of class $\nu$ of $\underline{A}_0(0, 0, z_5)$ such that

$\sum_{i=1}^{m} |M_i| = V \restriction \mathbf{G}_k(\underline{A}_0(0, 0, z_5))$

and $|M_i|$ is stationary in $\underline{A}_0(0, 0, z_5)$ for $i = 1, \ldots, m$. Furthermore, by using the estimates of part 3 in part 2, we see that if $B_i = M_i \cap \partial\underline{B}(0, z_5)$ for each $i = 1, \ldots, m$, then

$\partial M_i = B_i$, $i = 1, \ldots, m$.

By 2.7, for each $i = 1, \ldots, m$, $|M_i|$ is stationary in $\underline{U}(0, z_5)$; and by 2.6 and part 2,

$\|M_i\| \, \underline{U}(0, z_5) \leq \alpha(k)(1 + \underline{c}_1) \, z_5^k.$

Thus by 2.4(8), $0$ is a regular point of $M_i$. It follows that $0$ is a regular point of $\operatorname{spt}\|V\|$ by 7.3(1).
4ef2774c22b5.notes: Pitts 7.12. The final regularity theorem, joining with the existence theorem 4.11.

61acbaa3902a.name: Regularity Theorem for Morse-Tompkins-Shiffman Surfaces
61acbaa3902a.statement: If $2 \leq k \leq 5$, $C \in \mathbf{I}_{k-1}(\mathbb{R}^n)$ with $\partial C = 0$, and $T_1, T_2$ are locally mass minimizing fillings of $C$, then the varifold $V$ from 4.12 satisfies: $\operatorname{spt}\|V\| \setminus \operatorname{spt} C$ is a $k$-dimensional real analytic submanifold of $\mathbb{R}^{k+1}$.

40824feea393.name: Sweepout Construction
40824feea393.statement: On a surface $M$ diffeomorphic to $S^2$, orthogonal projection onto an axis gives a family of closed curves $\varphi(t) = M \cap P^{-1}(a + t(b-a))$ parametrized by $t \in I$, sweeping out all of $M$. The width $\mathbf{L}(\varphi) = \sup\{\operatorname{length}(\varphi(t)) : 0 \leq t \leq 1\}$ measures the longest curve in the family.
40824feea393.notes: Pitts 1.1(1). The basic sweepout construction motivating the min-max variational calculus.

cd63ff4083ee.name: Three-Legged Starfish Singularity
cd63ff4083ee.statement: On $M$ diffeomorphic to $S^2$ but metrized as a three-legged starfish (Almgren [AF2, p.15-8]), one critical path $\psi$ has a critical curve $\psi(c)$ shaped like a figure eight with a singularity at the point of intersection. This shows that critical surfaces produced by min-max methods may have essential singularities, justifying the use of integral currents rather than manifolds.
cd63ff4083ee.notes: Pitts 1.1(4). Due to Almgren. Motivates working with surfaces at least as general as integral currents.

38b232abd30e.name: Uniqueness Problem on the Sphere
38b232abd30e.statement: On $M = S^2$, there are two difficulties with selecting a minimal surface from a critical path. (1) Technical: a critical path may have many curves at the critical level $2\pi$ that are not minimal (solved by 4.3(2)). (2) Generic: by composing with rotations $\Phi(t) \in \mathrm{SO}(3)$, one can construct a critical path whose critical set contains every great circle on $S^2$. The problem is to find one suitable surface among many competitors.
38b232abd30e.notes: Pitts 1.1(5). Illustrates the need for refined selection of critical surfaces.

f9b07dafe757.name: Torus Example: Why Varifolds
f9b07dafe757.statement: On a torus $M$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$, one constructs a critical path $\psi : I \to \widetilde{\mathcal{Z}}_1(M)$ with $\mathbf{L}(\psi) = 4\pi$ and $\lim_{t \to 1/3} \mathbf{M}(\psi(t)) = 4\pi$, but $\psi(1/3) = 0$ as a current. The critical surface vanishes as a current but is recovered as a varifold: $V = \lim_{t \to 1/3} \psi(t)$ in the varifold topology is the generating circle with multiplicity two. This demonstrates why varifolds are necessary — mass is continuous in the varifold topology but only lower-semicontinuous in the flat topology on currents.
f9b07dafe757.notes: Pitts 1.1(6). The key motivating example for introducing varifolds into the variational calculus.

c65431996d25.name: Almost Minimizing on the Three-Legged Starfish
c65431996d25.statement: Revisiting the three-legged starfish: for any point $q$ on the figure-eight critical surface $V$ except the intersection point, there is a neighborhood $U$ such that $V$ is almost minimizing in $U$. The approximating cycles $T_j$ from the critical path are not themselves minimal, but for each $T_j$ one can construct $S_j$ locally area minimizing in $U$ via a mass-nonincreasing homotopy, with $\lim_j(\mathbf{M}(T_j) - \mathbf{M}(S_j)) = 0$. At the intersection point, however, there exists $\varepsilon > 0$ such that mass can always be decreased by $\varepsilon$, so $V$ is not almost minimizing there.
c65431996d25.notes: Pitts 1.2. Gives the geometric intuition for the definition of almost minimizing (3.1).

61709f7f78a2.name: Four-Legged Starfish: Bound on Exceptional Points
61709f7f78a2.statement: On a four-legged starfish $M$ with a plausible critical path $\varphi$ whose critical curve has two self-intersections, one explicitly constructs a second path $\psi$ homotopic to $\varphi$ with $\mathbf{L}(\psi) < \mathbf{L}(\varphi)$. The construction proceeds by sequentially 'opening up' each figure-eight via mass-decreasing homotopies. This shows $\varphi$ was not actually critical, and the true critical surface has at most one self-intersection. In general, the number of exceptional points where almost minimizing fails has an a priori upper bound depending on dimension (cf. 4.9, 4.10, 4.13).
61709f7f78a2.notes: Pitts 1.2. Motivates the covering lemma (4.8-4.9) and the combinatorial argument in 4.10.

ac9376e6beb7.name: Catenoid Decomposition of Stable Surfaces
ac9376e6beb7.statement: For two parallel circles $B_t$ of radius 1 at height $\pm t$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$, three minimal surfaces span $B_t$ for small $t$: the two parallel disks $D_t$ (stable), the small catenoid $S_t$ (stable, area $\to 0$), and the large catenoid $U_t$ (unstable, area $\to 2\pi$). As $t \to 0$, $D_t$ lies near the unit disk $D$ and almost covers it twice — it has a two-sheeted decomposition over $D$. Neither $S_t$ (stable but far from $D$) nor $U_t$ (near $D$ but unstable) admits such a decomposition. This illustrates the decomposition theorem (6.3): the behavior of $D_t$ is typical of stable hypersurfaces near a cone.

c17618179a3b.name: Scherk Surface and Stability Subtlety
c17618179a3b.statement: Scherk's second minimal surface $S_1 = \{(x,y,z) : \sin z = \sinh x \cdot \sinh y\}$ is a connected minimal surface of infinite genus. The rescaled surfaces $S_r = \{(x,y,z) : \sin rz = \sinh rx \cdot \sinh ry\}$ converge as $r \to \infty$ to the union of two orthogonal coordinate planes (as varifolds). This shows that a sequence of regular embedded minimal surfaces can converge to a singular varifold (two intersecting planes), illustrating the subtlety of the stability hypothesis in the decomposition theorem.
c17618179a3b.notes: Pitts 1.3(2). Due to K. Steffen. Shows limits of regular minimal surfaces can be singular.

2cb834d66287.name: Topology of Critical Surfaces on Flat Torus
2cb834d66287.statement: On the flat torus $M = \mathbb{R}^3/G$ (where $G$ is the integer lattice), the preimage $f^{-1}(S)$ of any 2-sphere $S \subset M$ under the covering map $f : \mathbb{R}^3 \to M$ is a disjoint union of 2-spheres in $\mathbb{R}^3$. Since $\mathbb{R}^3$ has no compact minimal submanifolds, there are no minimal spheres on $M$. This shows that the ambient manifold can impose extrinsic restrictions on the topology of critical surfaces, though in general there is no known method to prescribe the genus a priori.
2cb834d66287.notes: Pitts 1.4. Shows topology of the ambient manifold constrains the topology of minimal surfaces.

2e9a3d1e26e7.name: Stationarity of Comparison Surfaces
2e9a3d1e26e7.statement:
Combining (2) and <objref id="86b44c8a9da4"></objref>, we see that $V^*$ is stationary in $U$, although as far as we know $V^*$ might be quite different from $V$ in $K$. This is a remarkable fact that the property of being stationary endures through the construction <objref id="f355b39bb6eb"></objref>. We often have occasion to use it.

We also note that (4) will be critical in the regularity theory, because in certain dimensions the currents $T$ will have no singularities in $\operatorname{Int}(K)$. This is developed in Chapter 7.
2e9a3d1e26e7.notes: Pitts 3.11 Remark. Stationarity persists through the comparison construction.

45392be164dd.name: Non-uniqueness of Tangent Cones
45392be164dd.statement: This theorem does not say that the varifold tangent cones of an almost minimizing varifold are unique at each point. This technical difficulty is dealt with in <objref id="4ef2774c22b5"></objref>, after much of the regularity theory has been established.
45392be164dd.notes: Pitts 7.8 Remark. Tangent cone uniqueness is not proved until 7.12.

28f54c6ec84f.name: Normed Linear Space
28f54c6ec84f.statement: A **normed linear space** (or normed vectorspace) is a vectorspace $Y$ equipped with a norm $|\cdot| : Y \to [0, \infty)$ satisfying the usual axioms of positivity, homogeneity, and the triangle inequality.
28f54c6ec84f.notes: Basic functional analysis prerequisite. Used throughout Pitts 2.1.

40fdb7082e8b.name: Submanifold (with or without boundary)
40fdb7082e8b.statement: Throughout, $M$ denotes an $m$-dimensional $C^\nu$ <objref id="de020bf99ea4">submanifold</objref> of $\mathbb{R}^n$, either without boundary or a <objref id="496ce917fbe8">submanifold with boundary</objref>.
40fdb7082e8b.notes: Pitts 2.1(4). Umbrella concept unifying submanifold and submanifold with boundary.

881538e9b20a.name: Continuous Function
881538e9b20a.statement: A function $f : X \to Y$ between topological spaces is **continuous** if $f^{-1}(U)$ is open in $X$ for every open set $U \subset Y$. Equivalently, for metric spaces, $f$ is continuous at $p$ if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $\delta > 0$ such that $|f(x) - f(p)| < \varepsilon$ whenever $|x - p| < \delta$.
881538e9b20a.notes: Basic topology/analysis prerequisite. Corresponds to class C^0.

ebdf284f0e5e.name: Compactness
ebdf284f0e5e.statement: A subset $K$ of a topological space is **compact** if every open cover of $K$ admits a finite subcover. In $\mathbb{R}^n$, compactness is equivalent to being closed and bounded (Heine–Borel).
ebdf284f0e5e.notes: Basic topology prerequisite. Used throughout Pitts.

ac06488833f9.name: Inner Product Space
ac06488833f9.statement: An **inner product space** is a <objref id="28f54c6ec84f">normed linear space</objref> $(V, \langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle)$ equipped with an inner product $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle : V \times V \to \mathbb{R}$ (or $\mathbb{C}$) that is bilinear (or sesquilinear), symmetric (or conjugate-symmetric), and positive definite. The norm is induced by $|v| = \langle v, v \rangle^{1/2}$.
ac06488833f9.notes: Basic linear algebra prerequisite. Specializes normed linear space with additional inner product structure.

5956f657d327.name: Finite Dimensional
5956f657d327.statement: A module (or vectorspace) $V$ over a field $\mathbb{F}$ is **finite dimensional** if it admits a finite basis, i.e., there exist $v_1, \ldots, v_n \in V$ such that every $v \in V$ can be uniquely written as $v = \sum_{i=1}^n a_i v_i$ with $a_i \in \mathbb{F}$.
5956f657d327.notes: Basic linear algebra prerequisite. Mixin property (Prop): an orthogonal condition that can be combined with various algebraic/geometric structures (e.g., inner product space, normed space). Analogous to a Lean 4 Prop typeclass.

a8aeb8e5a15c.name: Inner Product on Hom
a8aeb8e5a15c.statement:
For $f, g \in$ <objref id="a03a9f4bd2cb">$\operatorname{Hom}(V, W)$</objref>, the **inner product** is

$$
f \cdot g = \operatorname{trace}(f^* \circ g).
$$

c031051e9e9a.name: Hilbert-Schmidt Norm
c031051e9e9a.statement:
For $f \in$ <objref id="a03a9f4bd2cb">$\operatorname{Hom}(V, W)$</objref>, the **Hilbert–Schmidt norm** is

$$
|f| = (f \cdot f)^{1/2}.
$$

247ceb818b0e.name: Operator Norm
247ceb818b0e.statement:
For $f \in$ <objref id="a03a9f4bd2cb">$\operatorname{Hom}(V, W)$</objref>, the **operator norm** is

$$
\|f\| = \sup\{|f(v)| : v \in V \text{ and } |v| = 1\}.
$$

ae1bdca7b0f4.name: General Linear Group
ae1bdca7b0f4.statement:
The **general linear group** $\mathbf{GL}(n)$ is the group of all invertible linear maps $\mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^n$, i.e.,

$$
\mathbf{GL}(n) = \{f \in \operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb{R}^n, \mathbb{R}^n) : f \text{ is invertible}\}.
$$

Here <objref id="a03a9f4bd2cb">$\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb{R}^n, \mathbb{R}^n)$</objref> is the space of linear mappings.
ae1bdca7b0f4.notes: Basic linear algebra. GL(n) ⊂ Hom(R^n, R^n).

0e7a726ea91a.name: Affine Group
0e7a726ea91a.statement: The **affine group** $\mathbf{A}(n)$ is the group of all affine isomorphisms of $\mathbb{R}^n$, i.e., maps of the form $x \mapsto Ax + b$ where $A \in$ <objref id="ae1bdca7b0f4">$\mathbf{GL}(n)$</objref> and $b \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
0e7a726ea91a.notes: Basic geometry. A(n) contains GL(n) as the subgroup of linear maps (b = 0).

494f1b0f972f.name: Grassmann Bundle
494f1b0f972f.statement:
Whenever $M$ is a <objref id="de020bf99ea4">$C^1$ submanifold</objref> of $\mathbb{R}^n$, we define the Grassmann bundle,

$$
\mathbf{G}_k(M) = M \times \mathbf{G}(n,k) \cap \{(x,S) : S \subset \operatorname{Tan}(M,x)\}.
$$

Here <objref id="ad4bed39adb7">$\mathbf{G}(n,k)$</objref> is the Grassmann manifold and <objref id="403ee09e952f">$\operatorname{Tan}(M,x)$</objref> is the tangent cone.

b129c86472a6.name: Kernel
b129c86472a6.statement:
For a linear map $f \in$ <objref id="a03a9f4bd2cb">$\operatorname{Hom}(V, W)$</objref>, the **kernel** of $f$ is

$$
\ker(f) = \{v \in V : f(v) = 0\}.
$$

d27b8d4a727d.name: Grassmann Projection Identification
d27b8d4a727d.statement:
We frequently identify $S \in$ <objref id="ad4bed39adb7">$\mathbf{G}(n,k)$</objref> with the orthogonal projection onto $S$ in <objref id="a03a9f4bd2cb">$\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb{R}^n, \mathbb{R}^n)$</objref>. Under this identification,

$$
S^\perp = \ker(S) \in \mathbf{G}(n,n-k).
$$

For each $S, T \in \mathbf{G}(n,k)$ and $x, y \in \mathbb{R}^n$:

$$
S - T = T^\perp - S^\perp, \quad \|S - T\| = \|S^\perp - T^\perp\|,
$$

$$
|(x,S) - (y,T)|^2 = |x - y|^2 + |S - T|^2,
$$

$$
0 \leq \|S - T\| \leq 1, \quad \|S - T\| = 1 \iff S \cap T^\perp \neq \{0\}.
$$
d27b8d4a727d.notes: Pitts 2.1(12). Follows from identifying G(n,k) with orthogonal projections in Hom(R^n, R^n) (cf. §2.1(6)).

9a32332d09ce.name: Lebesgue Measure
9a32332d09ce.statement:
For each $k \geq 1$ we denote by $\mathcal{L}^k$ the usual Lebesgue $k$ dimensional measure over $\mathbb{R}^k$ and set

$$
\alpha(k) = \mathcal{L}^k(\mathbf{B}(0,1)).
$$
9a32332d09ce.notes: Pitts 2.1(13). α(k) is the volume of the unit k-ball.

57e7976fb813.name: Weak Compactness of Radon Measures
57e7976fb813.statement:
If $\mathcal{Q}$ is a family of open sets whose union is $X$ and $B$ is a nonnegative, real-valued function on $\mathcal{Q}$, then

$$
\{\mu : \mu \text{ is a radon measure and } \mu(G) \leq B(G) \text{ whenever } G \in \mathcal{Q}\}
$$

is <objref id="ebdf284f0e5e">compact</objref> with respect to the <objref id="a12fe042f002">weak topology</objref> (cf. <objref id="af3f51d44df5">[FH1, 2.5.19]</objref>).
57e7976fb813.notes: Pitts 2.1(14a). [AW1, 2.6], [FH1, 2.5.19]. Formalization notes: hypothesis involves family of open sets (IsOpen), open cover (⋃ Q = X), and nonnegative real-valued bound (0 ≤ B G). These are type constraints in Lean 4, not standalone definitions.

585ee183ea75.name: Weak Convergence of Radon Measures
585ee183ea75.statement:
Let $\mu, \mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots$ be <objref id="7bf4252639fd">radon measures</objref> on $X$.

(i) $\lim \mu_i = \mu$ if and only if $\lim \mu_i(f) = \mu(f)$ for all $f \in$ <objref id="2638c73280ec">$\mathcal{K}(X)$</objref>.

(ii) $\lim \mu_i = \mu$ if and only if $\liminf \mu_i(G) \geq \mu(G)$ whenever $G$ is open and $\limsup \mu_i(K) \leq \mu(K)$ whenever $K$ is <objref id="ebdf284f0e5e">compact</objref>.

(iii) If $\lim \mu_i = \mu$, then $\lim \mu_i(A) = \mu(A)$ whenever <objref id="032a5beae62d">$\operatorname{Clos}(A)$</objref> is compact and $\mu($<objref id="1a9e4ca7df7d">$\operatorname{Bdry}(A)$</objref>$) = 0$.
585ee183ea75.notes: Pitts 2.1(14b). Characterizations of weak convergence of radon measures.

e24bfaf96c5e.name: Locally Compact Hausdorff Space
e24bfaf96c5e.statement: A topological space $X$ is **locally compact Hausdorff** if it is Hausdorff (distinct points have disjoint neighborhoods) and every point has a <objref id="ebdf284f0e5e">compact</objref> neighborhood.

dd64e059be89.name: Borel Measure
dd64e059be89.statement:
A **Borel measure** on a topological space $X$ is a measure defined on the $\sigma$-algebra of Borel sets (the smallest $\sigma$-algebra containing all open sets). It is **regular** if for every measurable set $A$,

$$
\mu(A) = \inf\{\mu(U) : U \supset A,\, U \text{ open}\} = \sup\{\mu(K) : K \subset A,\, K \text{ compact}\}.
$$

a12fe042f002.name: Weak Topology
a12fe042f002.statement: The **weak topology** on a space of <objref id="7bf4252639fd">radon measures</objref> is the coarsest topology such that $\mu \mapsto \mu(f)$ is <objref id="881538e9b20a">continuous</objref> for every $f \in$ <objref id="2638c73280ec">$\mathcal{K}(X)$</objref>.
a12fe042f002.notes: Basic functional analysis. Also called vague topology or weak-* topology on measures.

032a5beae62d.name: Closure
032a5beae62d.statement: The **closure** of a set $A$ in a topological space $X$, denoted $\operatorname{Clos}(A)$, is the smallest closed set containing $A$.

1a9e4ca7df7d.name: Boundary (Topological)
1a9e4ca7df7d.statement: The **boundary** of a set $A$ in a topological space $X$, denoted $\operatorname{Bdry}(A)$, is $\operatorname{Clos}(A) \setminus \operatorname{Int}(A)$, the set of points in the closure but not the interior.

35f7a8a23c92.name: Open Set
35f7a8a23c92.statement: A subset $U$ of a topological space $X$ is **open** if it belongs to the topology on $X$. In a metric space, $U$ is open if for every $p \in U$ there exists $r > 0$ such that $\mathbf{B}(p, r) \subset U$.
35f7a8a23c92.notes: Basic topology prerequisite. In Lean 4/Mathlib: IsOpen.

f517b1458fce.name: Lower Density
f517b1458fce.statement:
For each measure $\mu$ over $\mathbb{R}^n$ and each $p \in \mathbb{R}^n$, the $k$ **dimensional lower density of $\mu$ at $p$** is

$$
\Theta^k_*(\mu, p) = \liminf_{r \to 0+} \frac{\mu(\mathbf{B}(p, r))}{\alpha(k)\, r^k}.
$$

Here <objref id="c9a2001d7514">$\mathbf{B}(p,r)$</objref> is the open ball and <objref id="9a32332d09ce">$\alpha(k)$</objref> is the volume of the unit $k$-ball.

8d57f403346a.name: Density
8d57f403346a.statement:
In case the <objref id="90559cf74042">upper density</objref> and <objref id="f517b1458fce">lower density</objref> are equal, their common value is the $k$ **dimensional density**

$$
\Theta^k(\mu, p).
$$

f05df47698e6.name: Submanifolds and Rectifiability
f05df47698e6.statement:
If $M$ is a $k$ dimensional <objref id="de020bf99ea4">submanifold</objref> of class 1 of $\mathbb{R}^n$, then <objref id="75ed26c2a8de">$\mathcal{H}^k$</objref> $\llcorner M$ is the same appropriate intrinsic measure on $M$ as that obtained by any other reasonable method (for example, from the volume form associated with the induced Riemannian metric on $M$). If $\mathcal{H}^k(M) < \infty$, then $M$ is obviously <objref id="2666ddfa91d3">$(\mathcal{H}^k, k)$ rectifiable</objref>. On the other hand, according to <objref id="af3f51d44df5">[FH1, 3.2.29]</objref>, $S \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ is $(\mathcal{H}^k, k)$ rectifiable provided

$$
\mathcal{H}^k\Big(\bigcup_{j=1}^\infty M_j \sim S\Big) = 0
$$

for some sequence $M_1, M_2, \ldots$ of $k$ dimensional submanifolds of class 1 of $\mathbb{R}^n$ whose union has finite $\mathcal{H}^k$ measure. Consequently, sets which are $(\mathcal{H}^k, k)$ rectifiable and $\mathcal{H}^k$ measurable are generalizations of $k$ dimensional submanifolds of class 1.
